title: Pine and Plain Rectangle Coffee Table
summary: A flat-panel coffee table with solid pine legs and a full-width lower shelf split into two storage bays — clean lines, no aprons to fuss with.
description:
This table skips the usual apron-and-leg framework and builds instead from flat glued-up panels: a solid top, two panel legs, and a panel shelf below, joined with pocket holes. That construction is what gives it the plain, slab-sided look the name promises — there's no visible joinery to distract from the surfaces.

The shelf below is split by a center divider into two roughly two-thirds/one-third bays, which is a nice place to stash baskets or magazines without needing drawers. Get the divider and dividers' positions marked before you glue anything — the shelf panel has to land square between the legs or the whole lower half looks crooked from the front even if it's structurally fine.

Pocket holes are the recommended joinery throughout, but brad nails and glue or straight screws work too — you'll just need to fill or hide the fastener holes on the visible faces if you go that route.

tools.note: Alternative to pocket holes if you don't mind hiding a few fastener holes.

materials.name: 3/4" x 24" pine project panel, 3 ft
materials.note: Cut down for the second leg and the shelf panel.

cutList.note: Measure and cut to fit — spaced for a roughly 2/3 : 1/3 split of the shelf, about 15" for the smaller opening.

steps.title: Attach the legs to the top
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes on the top edges of the two leg panels and attach them to the underside of the top with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Brad nails and glue or straight 2" screws work too, but you'll need to hide those fastener holes on the visible faces.

steps.title: Attach the center support
steps.body: Measure and cut the center divider to fit snugly between the legs, then attach it with pocket holes, or with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails.

steps.title: Place the shelf dividers
steps.body: Position the two smaller dividers as desired — a 2/3 : 1/3 split (about 15" for the smaller opening) reads well, but this is the one place to trust your eye over an exact number.

steps.title: Attach the shelf
steps.body: Drill pocket holes in the bottom edge of the shelf panel first if you're using them. Apply glue to the top edges of the 1x12 dividers and the end edges of the shelf, set the shelf panel down on the dividers, and attach it with nails or pocket screws.

steps.title: Attach the bottom supports
steps.body: Attach the 1x3 bottom supports along the underside, best done with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws so the fasteners stay hidden on the inside. Brad nails or 2" self-tapping screws are the fallback option.

images.alt: A flat-panel pine coffee table with a two-bay storage shelf underneath
